lens.included_activities: Independent LMM contract logging operators that repeatedly cut, process, yard, load, and where contracted transport timber or produce field chips for external timberland owners, mills, forest managers, or public agencies.
lens.excluded_activities: Timber-tract owners selling standing timber, firms primarily taking timber-price ownership risk rather than charging for harvesting, vertically integrated mill crews, captive forestry units, one-person lifestyle operators, landholding shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat tract- or volume-based harvesting work under timber-sale, mill-supply, stewardship, or service contracts, with revenue commonly tied to harvested tons, board feet, loads, acres, machine time, or fixed project scope.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 113310 mixes outsourced contract harvesting with timber buyers and vertically integrated crews that earn a commodity spread or serve a captive owner. The lens keeps independent repeat-service contractors so the revenue and pass-through analysis is coherent.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader logging-worker occupation mix rather than payroll-weighted tasks at eligible contract loggers: 30,900 of 44,300 workers were equipment operators, with smaller groups of fallers and graders/scalers. I map dispatch, tract and route planning, production optimization, machine-health triage, bid preparation, safety documentation, invoicing, and some grading support as exposed; felling, machine operation, rigging, loading, maintenance, and terrain judgment remain physical and safety-critical. Including workers employed outside 113310 may overstate exposure, while omitting owner-manager administration may understate it.
a.rationale: Logging is already highly mechanized, but most wages remain attached to operating and maintaining heavy equipment in variable terrain; AI chiefly augments planning, coordination, monitoring, and office work.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries, telematics, dispatch logs, and administrative time studies from independent contractors across the South, Northeast, Lake States, and West would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: Occupation data include workers employed outside logging firms.
Regional harvesting systems and terrain create materially different task mixes.

rho.rationale: Dispatch, machine and truck coordination, bid support, maintenance alerts, documentation, and geospatial boundary control are implementable within five years. Remote connectivity, fragmented equipment fleets, safety validation, weather, tract variability, customer systems, capital constraints, and scarce technical staff slow deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Controlled multi-crew deployments reporting labor hours, truck wait time, fuel, utilization, boundary deviations, maintenance downtime, safety incidents, and administrative hours would move the range.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
The Forest Service coordination project demonstrates a workflow but not broad commercial AI adoption.

e.rationale: Independent contractors with repeat mill, landowner, or public-agency work fit, but timber buyers, integrated mill crews, owner-operators below the band, firms with excessive principal commodity risk, highly customer-dependent crews, and operations above the band do not. The supplied estimate of 28 LMM-band firms is useful but does not identify business model or transferability.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking SUSB-band candidates to customer contracts, timber ownership, mill affiliation, crew and equipment ownership, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and owner dependence would establish eligibility.
caveats: No current public source measures the eligible service share.
Contractor, timber buyer, dealer, and mill-affiliated models can coexist within one firm.
The supplied firm count is an estimate bridged from receipts and a sector margin.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence covers small employer firms generally rather than logging contractors. I translate older-owner succession pressure into a five-year qualifying-transfer probability, then discount equipment auctions, closures, family transfers, customer-driven crew absorption, and timber-rights transactions without control of an ongoing firm. Equipment intensity and workforce scarcity may encourage strategic purchases, while owner dependence may cause closure instead of transfer.
s5.rationale: Succession and equipment replacement can trigger sales, but many small logging businesses dissolve or sell machines rather than transfer a durable operating company.
s5.change_evidence: Logging-association membership cohorts, equipment-auction records, lender portfolios, UCC filings, state logger-certification ownership changes, and broker transactions with an eligible-firm denominator would move the estimate.
caveats: Worker replacement demand is not evidence of company control transfers.
No denominator-based transfer series for contract loggers.

q.rationale: A contractor can retain near-term gains on fixed or unit-rate work by completing tracts with fewer delays, better equipment utilization, and lower administration. Competitive timber bidding, mill bargaining, transparent per-ton rates, fuel and haul adjustments, contract renewal, and landowner price sensitivity pass part of the benefit through.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample with bid method, unit rates, escalation clauses, fuel surcharges, productivity guarantees, renewal repricing, and contractor gross margins before and after technology adoption would directly test retention.
caveats: No public contract panel quantifies pass-through.
Timber buyers and fee contractors allocate commodity and productivity risk differently.

d5.bridge: BLS projects logging-worker employment down 2 percent from 2024 to 2034, while Forest Service data show Southern pulpwood production fell from 59.0 million cords in 2021 to 56.0 million in 2022 and roundwood pulpwood fell to 41.6 million cords. I use these employment and one-product volume signals as a cautious proxy for broadly stable five-year service quantity, adjusting upward for labor-saving mechanization, wildfire thinning, salvage, biomass and mass-timber uses, and downward for paper decline, housing cycles, imports, mill closures, climate access limits, and regional concentration. Employment may understate service demand when productivity rises; Southern pulpwood may overstate decline for sawlog and western contractors.
d5.rationale: Wood harvesting remains necessary, but end-market and regional mix are cyclical and shifting; treatment and new wood uses offset weakness in some traditional products without guaranteeing volume for independent LMM crews.
d5.change_evidence: Annual national TPO roundwood removals by product and region, mill capacity additions and closures, housing and paper forecasts, federal and state treatment acres, contractor backlog, and customer retention cohorts would materially revise the range.
caveats: Employment combines demand and productivity effects.
The pulpwood observation is regional, product-specific, and dated.
Forest treatment appropriations do not guarantee merchantable contract volume.

o.rationale: Year-five logging quantity still requires an accountable operator to deploy crews and machines, assess site and tree hazards, follow harvest prescriptions, protect boundaries and soil, maintain equipment, load logs, and deliver physical material. Software and autonomy can reduce labor or change crew composition but do not remove operational custody and safety responsibility.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widely certified autonomous harvesting fleets operated remotely by mills, major customer self-performance, or contract structures that eliminate independent operating responsibility would move the estimate.
caveats: The accountable operator may become a larger integrated mill or fleet platform rather than an independent contractor.
This primitive excludes task automation already captured in a and rho.

narrative.executive_view: Contract logging has a modest AI opportunity in dispatch, telematics, planning, bidding, and administration, but the core work is dangerous, physical, capital-intensive, and terrain-specific. The dataset suggests only 28 LMM-band firms before eligibility screening, so individual-business diligence matters greatly.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely uses include tract and route planning, truck-machine coordination, production simulation, maintenance alerts, geospatial boundary checks, bid preparation, safety and environmental records, invoicing, and customer reporting. Felling, machine operation, rigging, loading, repair, and site hazard response remain operator work.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed and unit-rate contracts can preserve near-term productivity gains, but competitive bids, mill and landowner bargaining, transparent rates, fuel clauses, and renewals share value. Gains tied to fewer delays or better uptime may be harder for customers to observe and reprice immediately.
narrative.firm_availability: Independent repeat contractors fit, but the code also contains timber buyers and captive crews. A small estimated LMM pool, owner dependence, customer concentration, equipment debt, and the possibility of asset-only exits constrain transferable availability.
narrative.demand_durability: Physical timber harvesting persists across sawlogs, pulpwood, chips, salvage, thinning, and biomass. Housing and paper cycles, mill closures, imports, weather access, wildfire policy, mass timber, and regional resource conditions create a wide volume range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risks are a small estimated firm pool, business-model mixing, customer and mill concentration, timber cycles, equipment leverage and downtime, diesel, trucking, labor, severe safety exposure, weather, fire, soil and water rules, and inconsistent rural connectivity.
narrative.principal_driver: Machine and truck coordination, telematics, and administrative workflows can improve utilization in an already mechanized service.
narrative.principal_weakness: A small transferable pool serves volatile local wood markets while retaining irreducible safety, equipment, terrain, and customer-concentration risk.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: 113310 Logging
sources.supports: Census defines logging as cutting timber, cutting and transporting timber, or producing wood chips in the field, and separates timber-tract operations.

sources.title: Logging Workers
sources.supports: BLS reports 44,300 logging workers in 2024, including 30,900 equipment operators, describes physical harvesting and grading duties, and projects employment to decline 2 percent through 2034.

sources.title: Improving Mechanical Thinning and Biomass Transportation Efficiency
sources.supports: The Forest Service describes real-time machine and truck coordination, harvesting simulations, geospatial boundary control, and a project outcome of improving logging-operation production by 15 percent.

sources.title: 29 CFR 1910.266 Logging Operations
sources.supports: OSHA specifies safety practices for logging and requires site-specific hazard evaluation, supervisor consultation, machine control, and safe felling, yarding, loading, and transport.

sources.title: Southern Pulpwood Production, 2022
sources.supports: The Forest Service reports Southern pulpwood production declined from 59.0 million cords in 2021 to 56.0 million in 2022, with roundwood production at 41.6 million cords.

sources.title: Forest Resources of the United States, 2022
sources.supports: The 2026 Forest Service report provides national resource statistics on forest area, growth, removals, and timber products output and identifies the breadth of the physical resource base.

sources.title: What Loggers Are Noticing and Doing about Changing Winter Weather
sources.supports: Forest Service research reports fewer operable winter logging days, heavier equipment, workforce constraints, changing tract access, and adaptation by logging businesses in northern Minnesota.

sources.title: Selling Timber
sources.supports: University of Minnesota Extension describes timber-sale contracts, competitive bid solicitation, logger selection, harvest monitoring, and contract administration, supporting the customer and repricing model.

sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner
sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy for qualifying control transfers.
